title: Momentum: Rolling Down Hill
concept: Momentum speeds up gradient descent by accumulating past step directions to smooth out noise and roll over local valleys.

exercises.prompt: Recap: How does Learning Rate Decay improve gradient descent?

options.text: It gradually decreases step size so the model can settle precisely at the minimum.
It speeds up the steps near the end of training.

exercises.explain: Right! Shrunk steps let the model slide smoothly into the minimum. Now, what if the path is filled with little valleys and hills?

cards.title: The heavy bobsled
cards.body: Imagine a bobsled speeding down a track. If it hits a tiny bump or a small dip, it doesn't instantly stop or reverse — its accumulated speed carries it right over.

cards.title: Gradient Descent with Momentum
cards.body: Momentum is a technique where the optimizer remembers the direction of past steps and adds a fraction of them to the current step.

cards.title: A heavy ball
cards.body: Without momentum, the optimizer is like a weightless bead: it stops the second the slope is flat. With momentum, it's a heavy bowling ball rolling past minor bumps.

cards.title: Dampening the bounce
cards.body: In narrow valleys, SGD bounces uselessly side-to-side. Momentum averages out these cross-canyon bounces, cancelling them out while accelerating straight down the valley.

cards.title: It can overshoot
cards.body: While momentum is great for escaping shallow local minimums, a ball with too much speed can roll right out of the actual global minimum! Tuning is key.

cards.title: With vs. Without Momentum
cards.body: Comparing how the optimizer handles a noisy, ribbed valley floor:

right.text:
Smooths side bounces
Rolls right through dips

exercises.prompt: What physical property does the Momentum optimizer simulate?

options.text: Inertia: keeping the model moving in the general direction of previous steps.
Friction: slowing down the model near the start.
Gravity: pulling the model toward zero weights.
Elasticity: bouncing the weights off the maximum values.

exercises.explain: Yes! Momentum acts like inertia, using previous update directions to steer the current step.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Momentum makes the optimizer speed up along directions where gradients point consistently.
exercises.explain: True! If successive steps point in the same direction, momentum builds up, accelerating descent along that path.

exercises.sentence: Momentum averages out the side-to-side ___ to focus progress down the valley.
exercises.explain: Exactly! Side-to-side bounces (oscillations) cancel each other out, while forward speed is reinforced.

exercises.prompt: Which optimizer configuration is best suited to escape a shallow local minimum (a fake valley)?
exercises.explain: Momentum carries the optimizer up and out of shallow local dips, letting it continue searching for the lowest point.

exercises.prompt: Your loss landscape is a steep, narrow canyon. Without momentum, your optimizer bounces frantically between the left and right walls, barely moving forward. What changes when you enable momentum?

options.text: The wall-to-wall bounces cancel out, and the optimizer accelerates smoothly down the canyon floor.
The optimizer immediately gets stuck on the first wall it hits.
The model deletes the walls of the canyon automatically.
The learning rate is forced to zero.

exercises.explain: Spot on! Momentum averages out opposing oscillations and accelerates down consistent slopes. Next: how to customize steps for individual features!
